g.data-narration: Welcome to Logic fundamentals. We'll explore Propositional and Predicate Logic step by step.
Logic is the foundation of computer science. It enables circuit design, AI, database queries, and program verification.
g.text: • Circuit Design • Database Queries • AI & ML • Program Verification

g.data-narration: A proposition is a declarative statement that is either TRUE or FALSE, but never both. Let's see examples.
Five connectives combine propositions: Negation ¬, Conjunction ∧, Disjunction ∨, Implication →, and Biconditional ↔.
Truth tables show all possible truth value combinations. For n propositions, we need 2^n rows. Watch the patterns.
g.text: Truth tables list ALL combinations of truth values for propositions.
For 2 propositions (P, Q): 2² = 4 rows. For 3 propositions: 2³ = 8 rows.
∧ (AND): True only when BOTH are true
∨ (OR): True when AT LEAST one is true

g.data-narration: Implication P → Q is FALSE only when P is TRUE and Q is FALSE. This is the most common exam trap!
g.text: Implication (→) - THE KEY TO TRUTH TABLES
P → Q means "If P then Q"
Only FALSE when P=TRUE and Q=FALSE

g.data-narration: Tautology is always TRUE. Contradiction is always FALSE. Contingency depends on truth values.
De Morgan's Laws: ¬(P ∧ Q) ≡ ¬P ∨ ¬Q and ¬(P ∨ Q) ≡ ¬P ∧ ¬Q. Distribute negation and flip the connective.
Predicate logic extends propositional logic with predicates and variables. A predicate becomes a proposition when variables are assigned.
g.text: Predicate: Statement with variables that becomes proposition when values are assigned
Example: P(x) = "x is a prime number" → P(5) = True, P(4) = False

g.data-narration: Universal ∀ means 'for all' - statement must be true for every value. Existential ∃ means 'there exists' - at least one value satisfies.
g.text: "For all" — ∀x P(x) means P(x) is true for every x
"There exists" — ∃x P(x) means at least one x makes P(x) true

g.data-narration: Translation rule: 'Every' → ∀ with → (implication). 'Some/Exists' → ∃ with ∧ (conjunction). This is crucial for exams!
Rules of inference prove conclusions from premises. Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens are the most commonly used rules.
g.text: Rules of Inference - The Proof Tools

g.data-narration: Let's prove: Given 'If it rains, ground gets wet' and 'It rains', conclude 'Ground gets wet' using Modus Ponens.
g.text: Given: (1) R → W (If it rains, ground gets wet)
By Modus Ponens: ∴ W (Ground gets wet)

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Propositions are True/False statements. Five connectives build compound propositions. Implication is key in truth tables.

g.text: Proposition: declarative statement, True or False
P→Q is FALSE only when P=T, Q=F
